content.description: Past tenses do not always refer to past time. In some sentences and with some verbs or phrases we use a past tense to refer to the present or the future or to a general situation. These include sentences that contain:

rows: a second conditional
I'd buy that top if I **had** more cash on me.
*suppose / what if / imagine*

I know it's probably not going to happen but ***suppose** / **imagine*** I **became** a famous super model!
I know it's probably not going to happen but ***what if*** I **became** a famous super model?
*would rather* + you/he/she/we/they
I'd **rather you didn't come** with me to the fashion show tomorrow.
*it's (high/about) time*
**It's (high/about) time** that hats **came** back in fashion.
polite questions
**Did** you **want** me to send you our new catalogue when it comes out?
*wish / if only*
**If only / I wish I had** something to wear tonight.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
content.description:
*Suppose*, *what if* and *imagine* can also be followed by a present tense.
A present tense indicates that the situation is more likely to actually happen.
A past tense indicates that the situation is less likely to actually happen.
items: ✓ What if you **are** accepted into art college? (more likely to happen)
✓ What if you **were** accepted into art college? (less likely to happen)

content.description: We use different structures with *wish / if only*, depending on exactly what we want to express.

rows: Expressing wishes about the present, future or generally
*wish / if only* + past simple/continuous
I **wish** I **was studying** history of fashion instead of biology!
Expressing wishes about the past
*wish / if only* + past perfect simple/continuous
**If only I'd known** Burton's was having a sale last week.
Criticising other people or complaining about a situation now, in the future or generally
*wish / if only* + would
**If only** Henry **would** get a haircut!
Expressing hypothetical ability or permission now, in the future or generally
*wish / if only* + could
I **wish** I **could** fit into these jeans.
Expressing desires in a formal way
*wish* + full infinitive
I **wish to try on** this ball gown.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We do not usually say *If only / I wish I would* ...
✓ **If only / I wish I had** enough money to buy these shoes.
✓ **If only / I wish I could** afford these shoes.
✗ If only / I wish I **would** have enough money to buy these shoes.
To express desires about the future, we often use *hope*.
✓ I **hope** I **will** have enough money to buy these shoes next week.
✓ I **hope to have** enough money to buy these shoes next week.
✗ I **wish** I **will** have enough money to buy these shoes next week.

content.description: *Although*, *though* and *even though* are used to express contrast. *Even though* is more emphatic than *although* and *though*. *Though* is more informal than *although* and *even though*.

rows: *Although / Though / Even though* + subject + verb, subject + verb
**Although / Though / Even though** I wore a hat, I got sunburn.
subject + verb, *although / though / even though* + subject + verb
I got sunburn, **although / though / even though** I wore a hat.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: *Though* can also come at the end of a sentence.
✓ I wore a hat. I got sunburn, *though*.

content.description: *In spite of* and *despite* are used to express contrast. They mean exactly the same thing and take the same grammatical structures:

rows: *in spite of / despite* + -ing form, subject + verb
**In spite of / Despite** wearing a hat, I got sunburn.
*in spite of / despite* + the fact (that) + subject + verb, subject + verb
**In spite of / Despite** the fact (that) I wore a hat, I got sunburn.
*in spite of / despite* + noun, subject + verb
**In spite of / Despite** my hat, I got sunburn.

content.subtitle: Watch out!
items: We can also put *in spite of / despite* in the middle of the sentence.
✓ I got sunburn **in spite of / despite** wearing a hat.
We can also use the perfect -ing form to show that the action happened before the result.
✓ I got sunburn **in spite of / despite having** worn a hat.
With *in spite of / despite* + -ing form, the subject of the main verb must also carry out the action of the -ing verb.
✗ My sunburn was terrible **in spite of / despite** wearing a hat. (= My sunburn wore a hat!)

content.description: *However* and *nevertheless* are used to express contrast. *However* is formal. *Nevertheless* is even more formal.

examples: The fashion show was expensive to put on. **However / Nevertheless**, it did make a profit.
The fashion show was expensive to put on. It did make a profit, **however / nevertheless**.
The fashion show was expensive to put on. It did, **however / nevertheless**, make a profit.

content.description: *While* and *whereas* are used to contrast two different facts or ideas.

rows: *while / whereas* + subject + verb, subject + verb
**While / Whereas** jeans are worn by both men and women, blouses are worn only by women.
subject + verb, *while / whereas* + subject + verb
Blouses are worn only by women, **while / whereas** jeans are worn by both men and women.

sections.title: A: Unreal past forms
sections.instructions: Write the correct form of the verb in brackets.

items.sentence: Don't you think it's time you (tell) ...... Andrea the truth?
What if we (rent) ...... a villa by the sea for the summer?
I'd rather people (send) ...... me e-mails than (phone) ...... me.
Suppose you (be going to) ...... open a clothes shop. What kind of clothes would you stock?
It's time to (go) ...... now.
If you (have) ...... a time machine, which era would you want to go back to?
Sheila would come with us if she (not have to) ...... work tomorrow.
We'd rather you (not lend) ...... Brenda any more money - she's got to learn not to spend what she doesn't have.
It's high time I (leave) ......
What if we (get) ...... married? Would that make you happy?

sections.title: B: Error correction (Wishes)
sections.instructions: Each of the words or phrases in bold is incorrect. Rewrite them correctly.

items.sentence: I wish I **know** how to set the timer on this video.
'If only I **listened** to Hilary,' said Warren sadly. 'Now it's too late.'
I wish you **did** shut up for five minutes!
If only Charlie **can** get tomorrow off work.
Do you wish you **went** with Jack to the concert tomorrow night?
Excuse me. I wish **speaking** with the manager of this establishment immediately.
Albert wishes like anything that he **was driving** more slowly that night.
I wish I **would feel** well enough to eat something.
I **wish** I'll get my exam results sooner rather than later.
If only you **to listen** to what your teachers tell you!
It's about time you ...... thinkin about setting up your own business.
I'd really rather you ...... the kids drop bits of pizza all over my new sofa.
...... anything particular in mind or are you just browsing?
I wish Karen ...... that we're not trying to interfere in her life.
I'd have been able to say goodbye to Trish if only ...... to the airport in time.
Don't you sometimes wish you ...... invisible?
Suppose we ...... we could go and live on another planet. Would you want to go?
Do you wish ...... an official complaint?
Adrian was ...... enough money to buy a new computer for the school.
Don't you wish we ...... the exam a week on Monday and not tomorrow?

sections.title: D: Wish transformations
sections.instructions: Complete the sentences so that the meaning remains the same.

items.sentence: I don't like your criticising me all the time.
items.answer: I wish you wouldn't criticise me all the time.

items.sentence: It's awful - Sam doesn't know how I feel about him!
items.answer: If only Sam knew how I felt about him.

items.sentence: I'd love to be with you in Switzerland right now.
items.answer: I wish I were with you in Switzerland right now.

items.sentence: Frank regrets not taking the job when he had the chance.
items.answer: Frank wishes he had taken the job when he had the chance.

items.sentence: Wouldn't you like us to be standing in the middle of Times Square right now?
items.answer: Don't you wish we were standing in the middle of Times Square right now?

items.sentence: It's a shame I can't play chess as well as Steve.
items.answer: I wish I could play chess as well as Steve.

items.sentence: What would you do if you had a million euros?
items.answer: Suppose you had a million euros, what would you do?

items.sentence: I don't really want you to smoke in here.
items.answer: I'd rather you didn't smoke in here.

items.sentence: It's time for them to go to bed.
items.answer: It's high time they went to bed.

items.sentence: Although **F** Aesop's a vegetarian, he occasionally eats meat.
Despite **D** being a vegetarian, Aesop occasionally eats meat.
Despite the **H** fact that Aesop is a vegetarian, he occasionally eats meat.
Aesop is a vegetarian, **I** although he occasionally eats meat.
Despite his vegetarianism, **C** Aesop occasionally eats meat.
In spite **A** of being a vegetarian, Aesop occasionally eats meat.
Despite having **G** been a vegetarian all his life, Aesop occasionally eats meat.
Aesop is a vegetarian. However, he **B** does occasionally eat meat.
Aesop is a vegetarian. He does **E** occasionally eat meat.

sections.title: F: Key word transformation (Contrast)
sections.instructions: Complete each second sentence using the word given, so that it has a similar meaning to the first sentence. Write between two and five words in each gap.

items.sentence:
Although he was exhausted, Mike kept on climbing.
**fact** In ...... he was exhausted, Mike kept on climbing.

Sally had been sure she would win the music competition. She came third, however.
**been** Despite ...... would win the music competition, Sally came third.

Doug wasn't shortlisted for the job even though he had a great CV.
**spite** Doug wasn't shortlisted for the job ...... a great CV.

Planes can still take off and land but ferries have been cancelled.
**whereas** Ferries have been cancelled, ...... able to take off and land.

Despite having got a good degree, Sandra couldn't find a well-paid job.
**even** Sandra couldn't find a well-paid job ...... a good degree.

Even though he'd been robbed twice, Red still enjoyed living in the city.
**having** Red still enjoyed living in the city ...... twice.

It's a great film despite the plot being a little complicated.
**though** It's a great film, ...... a little complicated.

Despite looking everywhere, the kids failed to find the tennis ball.
**although** The kids failed to find the tennis ball ...... everywhere.

Pat really enjoyed the match even though she was beaten.
**of** In ......, Pat really enjoyed the match.

Despite their thorough investigation of the case, the police didn't manage to catch the culprit.
**investigated** Despite ......, the police didn't manage to catch the culprit.

sections.title: G: Error correction (extra words)
sections.instructions: Find the extra word in each line. There are 8 extra words. Click on the extra words to mark them.
sections.passage: I went to my first school dance last Saturday night and it was a disaster! [[If]] only [[that]] I'd thought about what I was going to wear a bit more carefully. Although [[being]] my best friend Jenny had told me it wasn't that kind of event, I'd decided to look really grown-up and wear a dress and high-heeled shoes. When I got there, I looked totally ridiculous and [[some]] people started laughing. Despite [[of]] this slight setback, I thought I'd hit the dance floor. If I could [[have]] survive that, I could survive anything. But even though [[that]] went wrong! One of my heels snapped - even [[if]] though my shoes were brand new - and I had to carry them to the side of the room. It was very embarrassing! [[Still]], despite the disastrous start [[was]], Craig Fielding came up to me and asked me if I wanted to go out with him next Saturday. Of course I said no, but he is cute! We'll see what happens!

sections.title: H: Cloze (Contrast and wishes)
sections.instructions: Write one word in each gap.

items.sentence: (1) ...... the fact that I try to be as upbeat as possible in this column, I have to say Milan has disappointed me.
If only we (2) ...... possibly go back to the glory days of fashion (ie last year) when skirts were short and pink was the new black.
Pink is still in this year, interestingly enough. (3) ...... it's only being used in accessories such as buckles, belts, braces and beads.
Brown - the must-have colour of the season - has never been my favourite colour, (4) ...... I have to say that Belinda Steven's all-brown paper beachwear was quite stunning.
That's in (5) ...... of its complete impracticality, of course.
It really is (6) ...... time that haute couture entered the real world just a tiny bit.
I know it's supposed to be more art than clothing, but suppose you actually (7) ...... the mistake of buying a Diego Fernandez baseball cap made of candle wax.

That's one walk in the sunshine and five thousand euros is dripping down your face! Now, I'm not saying I wish I (8) ...... stayed back in dull and grey London - the after-show parties were tremendous, as always.

But I do hope that next season I (9) ...... be able to report that the fashion world's got a grip on itself once more, and then - (10) ...... though none of us will ever be able to afford any of the items I describe in this column - at least we'll have something to dream about once more.
